info.structure: Part 45: Chapter 4, Sections 1–3. The story of Jānaśruti and Raikva of the cart, and the Saṃvarga-vidyā (doctrine of the Absorber).
info.format: Devanagari, IAST, English translation, and philosophical commentaries.

verses.devanagari: जानश्रुतिर्ह पौत्रायणः श्रद्धादेयो बहुदायी बहुपाक्य आस स ह सर्वत आवसथान्मापयांचक्रे सर्वत एव मेऽन्नमत्स्यन्तीति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: jānaśrutir ha pautrāyaṇaḥ śraddhādeyo bahudāyī bahupākya āsa sa ha sarvata āvasathān māpayāṃ cakre sarvata eva me 'nnam atsyantīti || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa was a giver with faith, a giver of much, a keeper of many kitchens. He had rest-houses built everywhere, thinking: 'Everywhere people will eat my food.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara presents the generous king whose charity, though noble, still carries the subtle pride of 'my food'. The story will humble him toward knowledge, which charity alone cannot buy.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The king's open-handedness is genuine dharma, the seed-bed of grace; yet the narrative shows that the Lord leads even the virtuous further — from giving to knowing.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva notes that meritorious works prepare but do not replace knowledge of the Lord; Jānaśruti's puṇya earns him the encounter that will instruct him.

verses.devanagari: अथ हंसा निशायामतिपेतुस्तद्धैवं हंसो हंसमभ्युवाद हो होऽयि भल्लाक्ष भल्लाक्ष जानश्रुतेः पौत्रायणस्य समं दिवा ज्योतिराततं तन्मा प्रसाङ्क्षीस्तत्त्वा मा प्रधाक्षीरिति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: atha haṃsā niśāyām atipetus tad dhaivaṃ haṃso haṃsam abhyuvāda ho ho 'yi bhallākṣa bhallākṣa jānaśruteḥ pautrāyaṇasya samaṃ divā jyotir ātataṃ tan mā prasāṅkṣīs tat tvā mā pradhākṣīr iti || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: Now one night some swans flew past, and one swan said to another: 'Ho there, short-sighted one! The light of Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa has spread like the sky. Do not touch it, lest it burn you!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The swans — sages or gods in bird form, says Śaṅkara — speak so the king may overhear. His 'light' is the lustre of merit; the dialogue is staged by providence to provoke his aspiration.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord employs even birds as messengers of grace; praise of the king's light is the bait that will draw him to a true knower.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Divine beings in swan form execute the Lord's plan, honouring merit while preparing its transcendence.

verses.devanagari: तमु ह परः प्रत्युवाच कम्वर एनमेतत्सन्तं सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tam u ha paraḥ pratyuvāca kam vara enam etat santaṃ sayugvānam iva raikvam āttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: The other replied: 'Come now — who is this fellow of whom you speak as if he were Raikva of the cart?' 'And what sort of man is this Raikva of the cart?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The swan's retort dethrones royal merit before the silent knower: all the king's glory does not compare with Raikva. Knowledge outshines works.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: True greatness lies hidden with the Lord's knower, not in public charity; the swan redirects the king's gaze.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The hierarchy of worth is divinely proclaimed: the knower of the Lord ranks above the wealthiest giver.

verses.devanagari: यथा कृतायविजितायाधरेयाः संयन्त्येवमेनं सर्वं तदभिसमैति यत्किञ्च प्रजाः साधु कुर्वन्ति यस्तद्वेद यत्स वेद स मयैतदुक्त इति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yathā kṛtāya vijitāyādhareyāḥ saṃyanty evam enaṃ sarvaṃ tad abhisamaiti yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti yas tad veda yat sa veda sa mayaitad ukta iti || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: 'As the lower throws of the dice all pass to the winner of the kṛta (the highest throw), so whatever good people do, all of it accrues to him. He who knows what Raikva knows — it is of him I speak.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara's celebrated image: as all lesser dice-throws are contained in the highest, all merits are subsumed in the knower of Brahman. Knowledge gathers and surpasses every good work.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: All virtue ripens toward and is credited to the one who knows the Lord; the knower is the focal point of the world's goodness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The Lord assigns the fruit of scattered merits to His knower; knowledge of Him is the kṛta-throw that wins all.

verses.devanagari: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायण उपशुश्राव स ह संजिहान एव क्षत्तारमुवाचाङ्गारे ह सयुग्वानमिव रैक्वमात्थेति यो नु कथं सयुग्वा रैक्व इति ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tad u ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇa upaśuśrāva sa ha saṃjihāna eva kṣattāram uvācāṅgāre ha sayugvānam iva raikvam āttheti yo nu kathaṃ sayugvā raikva iti || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa overheard this. Rising at once, he said to his attendant: 'Friend, you speak of me as if I were Raikva of the cart!' — 'And what sort of man is this Raikva of the cart?'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The king, stung awake, repeats the swan's words — the first sign of the humility that qualifies one for knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Grace works: the king cannot rest until he finds the man greater than himself.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Hearing of a superior, the king seeks him — the right response to revealed hierarchy.

verses.devanagari: यथा कृतायविजितायाधरेयाः संयन्त्येवमेनं सर्वं तदभिसमैति यत्किञ्च प्रजाः साधु कुर्वन्ति यस्तद्वेद यत्स वेद स मयैतदुक्त इति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: yathā kṛtāya vijitāyādhareyāḥ saṃyanty evam enaṃ sarvaṃ tad abhisamaiti yat kiñca prajāḥ sādhu kurvanti yas tad veda yat sa veda sa mayaitad ukta iti || 6 ||
verses.english_translation: (The attendant repeated:) 'As the lower throws of the dice all pass to the winner of the kṛta, so whatever good people do, all of it accrues to him. He who knows what Raikva knows — it is of him I speak.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The teaching is repeated verbatim — scripture's way of fixing the dice-image in the seeker's mind.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Repetition confirms the doctrine: the knower absorbs all merit.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The restatement certifies the truth the king must act upon.

verses.devanagari: स ह क्षत्तान्विष्य नाविदमिति प्रत्येयाय तं होवाच यत्रारे ब्राह्मणस्यान्वेषणा तदेनमर्च्छेति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: sa ha kṣattānviṣya nāvidam iti pratyeyāya taṃ hovāca yatrāre brāhmaṇasyānveṣaṇā tad enam arccheti || 7 ||
verses.english_translation: The attendant searched and returned saying, 'I did not find him.' The king said: 'Where one searches for a knower of Brahman — there seek him (in solitude, in the wilds).'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Knowers of Brahman are not found in palaces but where the world does not look — the king himself supplies the clue, showing his ripening discernment.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord's men dwell hidden; the seeker must look with reverence, away from pomp.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The true knower shuns display; he is sought where worldly eyes do not go.

verses.devanagari: सोऽधस्ताच्छकटस्य पामानं कषमाणमुपोपविवेश तं हाभ्युवाद त्वं नु भगवः सयुग्वा रैक्व इत्यहं ह्यरा३ इति ह प्रतिजज्ञे स ह क्षत्ताविदमिति प्रत्येयाय ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: so 'dhastāc chakaṭasya pāmānaṃ kaṣamāṇam upopaviveśa taṃ hābhyuvāda tvaṃ nu bhagavaḥ sayugvā raikva ity ahaṃ hy arā3 iti ha pratijajñe sa ha kṣattāvidam iti pratyeyāya || 8 ||
verses.english_translation: He found him beneath a cart, scratching his itch, and sat down near him, saying: 'Are you, revered sir, Raikva of the cart?' — 'Indeed I am!' he acknowledged. The attendant returned, saying: 'I have found him.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The greatest knower appears as the least of men — itching under a cart. Śaṅkara: outward state reveals nothing of inner attainment; the world's judgments are inverted in the realm of knowledge.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord hides His jewels in plain clay; reverence ('bhagavaḥ') is rightly offered despite appearances.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Raikva's lowly seat conceals exalted knowledge — rank before God owes nothing to circumstance.

verses.devanagari: तदु ह जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायणः षट्शतानि गवां निष्कमश्वतरीरथं तदादाय प्रतिचक्रमे तं हाभ्युवाद ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: tad u ha jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ ṣaṭśatāni gavāṃ niṣkam aśvatarīrathaṃ tad ādāya praticakrame taṃ hābhyuvāda || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: Then Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa took six hundred cows, a gold necklace, and a chariot drawn by mules, and went back to him, saying:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The king brings wealth to buy what wealth cannot buy — the first offering will be refused, teaching that knowledge is not merchandise.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Gifts express the disciple's earnestness, yet the teacher waits for the offering of self.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Material gifts alone cannot win the vidyā; the teacher tests the seeker's totality.

verses.devanagari: रैक्वेमानि षट्शतानि गवामयं निष्कोऽयमश्वतरीरथोऽनु म एतां भगवो देवतां शाधि यां देवतामुपास्स इति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: raikvemāni ṣaṭśatāni gavām ayaṃ niṣko 'yam aśvatarīratho 'nu ma etāṃ bhagavo devatāṃ śādhi yāṃ devatām upāssa iti || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: 'Raikva, here are six hundred cows, this necklace, this mule-chariot. Now teach me, revered sir, the deity you worship.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The request is right, but the approach is mercantile; the teacher's rebuff will purify it.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: He asks for the upāsya-devatā — the proper question; the manner must still ripen into surrender.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The seeker asks for the deity of meditation; teaching awaits fuller humility.

verses.devanagari: तमु ह परः प्रत्युवाचाह हारेत्वा शूद्र तवैव सह गोभिरस्त्विति तदु ह पुनरेव जानश्रुतिः पौत्रायणः सहस्रं गवां निष्कमश्वतरीरथं दुहितरं तदादाय प्रतिचक्रमे ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tam u ha paraḥ pratyuvācāha hāretvā śūdra tavaiva saha gobhir astv iti tad u ha punar eva jānaśrutiḥ pautrāyaṇaḥ sahasraṃ gavāṃ niṣkam aśvatarīrathaṃ duhitaraṃ tad ādāya praticakrame || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: But the other replied: 'Ah, take them away, śūdra! Keep the necklace and chariot together with the cows.' Then Jānaśruti Pautrāyaṇa came back again with a thousand cows, the necklace, the mule-chariot, and his daughter.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Śaṅkara explains 'śūdra' not literally but as 'one melting with grief (śuc)' or one approaching knowledge like a śūdra — with goods instead of qualification. The rebuke burns away the king's transactional spirit.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The harsh word tests and humbles; the king's return with his dearest — his daughter — shows surrender deepening into totality.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva reads the rebuke as gauging the seeker's perseverance; true desire for the Lord's knowledge survives insult.

verses.devanagari: तं हाभ्युवाद रैक्वेदं सहस्रं गवामयं निष्कोऽयमश्वतरीरथ इयं जायेयं ग्रामो यस्मिन्नास्सेऽन्वेव मा भगवः शाधीति ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: taṃ hābhyuvāda raikvedaṃ sahasraṃ gavām ayaṃ niṣko 'yam aśvatarīratha iyaṃ jāyeyaṃ grāmo yasminn āsse 'nv eva mā bhagavaḥ śādhīti || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: He said to him: 'Raikva, here are a thousand cows, this necklace, this mule-chariot, this maiden for a wife, and this village in which you sit. Now, revered sir, teach me!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Now everything is laid down — wealth, kin, dominion. When nothing is withheld, the disciple is ready; the gift of all signifies the gift of self.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Complete surrender (ātma-nikṣepa) at the teacher's feet is the gateway of grace; the Lord's knower now consents.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Total offering demonstrates fitness; the teacher, seeing it, will impart the saving doctrine.

verses.devanagari: तस्या ह मुखमुपोद्गृह्णन्नुवाचाजहारेमाः शूद्रानेनैव मुखेनालापयिष्यथा इति ते हैते रैक्वपर्णा नाम महावृषेषु यत्रास्मा उवास स तस्मै होवाच ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: tasyā ha mukham upodgṛhṇann uvācājahāremāḥ śūdrānenaiva mukhenālāpayiṣyathā iti te haite raikvaparṇā nāma mahāvṛṣeṣu yatrāsmā uvāsa sa tasmai hovāca || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: Raising her face toward him, Raikva said: 'He has brought all these, śūdra; by this face alone you would have made me speak!' Those villages among the Mahāvṛṣas where he lived are called Raikvaparṇa. And he said to him:

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The maiden's face — the emblem of the king's total relinquishment — unlocks the teaching. Śaṅkara: it is the completeness of surrender, not the items given, that 'makes the teacher speak'.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Acceptance of the bride seals the bond of teacher and disciple as kin; grace now flows freely.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The teacher accepts the offering as the Lord's arrangement and proceeds to instruct the now-qualified king.

sections.title: Saṃvarga-Vidyā — Wind and Breath, the Two Absorbers

verses.devanagari: वायुर्वाव संवर्गो यदा वा अग्निरुद्वायति वायुमेवाप्येति यदा सूर्योऽस्तमेति वायुमेवाप्येति यदा चन्द्रोऽस्तमेति वायुमेवाप्येति ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: vāyur vāva saṃvargo yadā vā agnir udvāyati vāyum evāpyeti yadā sūryo 'stam eti vāyum evāpyeti yadā candro 'stam eti vāyum evāpyeti || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: The wind, verily, is the absorber (saṃvarga). When fire goes out, it passes into the wind; when the sun sets, it passes into the wind; when the moon sets, it passes into the wind.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Raikva's doctrine: the wind gathers the cosmic gods into itself — a meditation on the One into which the many resolve, foreshadowing the dissolution of all into Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Vāyu's absorbing power is the Lord's own gathering energy at work in the cosmos; meditation on it honours His sustaining-and-withdrawing play.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: For Madhva, Vāyu is the Lord's foremost agent: the gods' resolution into him at their setting displays his unique rank under Viṣṇu.

verses.devanagari: यदाप उच्छुष्यन्ति वायुमेवापियन्ति वायुर्ह्येवैतान्सर्वान्संवृङ्क्ते इत्यधिदैवतम् ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: yadāpa ucchuṣyanti vāyum evāpiyanti vāyur hy evaitān sarvān saṃvṛṅkte ity adhidaivatam || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: When the waters dry up, they pass into the wind. For the wind absorbs them all. This with reference to the gods.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The cosmic side of the teaching is complete: all luminaries and elements return to wind, the unresting power of the manifest world.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The visible resolution of elements into Vāyu mirrors the final resolution of all into the Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Vāyu's universal absorption is delegated might, magnifying the Lord who empowers him.

verses.devanagari: अथाध्यात्मं प्राणो वाव संवर्गः स यदा स्वपिति प्राणमेव वागप्येति प्राणं चक्षुः प्राणं श्रोत्रं प्राणं मनः प्राणो ह्येवैतान्सर्वान्संवृङ्क्त इति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: athādhyātmaṃ prāṇo vāva saṃvargaḥ sa yadā svapiti prāṇam eva vāg apyeti prāṇaṃ cakṣuḥ prāṇaṃ śrotraṃ prāṇaṃ manaḥ prāṇo hy evaitān sarvān saṃvṛṅkta iti || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: Now with reference to the self: the breath, verily, is the absorber. When one sleeps, speech passes into the breath; the eye into the breath; the ear into the breath; the mind into the breath. For the breath absorbs them all.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: In sleep the faculties dissolve into breath — nightly proof that the many rest in a prior one. The meditation uses this daily dissolution as a window onto the final unity.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The inner absorber is the Lord's vital presence within; the senses' nightly return to prāṇa is their homage to the indweller.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Prāṇa — again Vāyu within the body — receives the senses in sleep, governing them as the Lord's deputy.

verses.devanagari: तौ वा एतौ द्वौ संवर्गौ वायुरेव देवेषु प्राणः प्राणेषु ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: tau vā etau dvau saṃvargau vāyur eva deveṣu prāṇaḥ prāṇeṣu || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: These, verily, are the two absorbers: the wind among the gods, the breath among the vital powers.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: One absorbing principle wears two names, cosmic and personal — the lesson of all Upaniṣadic correlation: what is without is within.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The twin absorbers are one minister of the Lord serving in two theatres.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Vāyu and prāṇa are one mighty soul in two offices, supreme among created beings.

verses.devanagari: अथ ह शौनकं च कापेयमभिप्रतारिणं च काक्षसेनिं परिविष्यमाणौ ब्रह्मचारी बिभिक्षे तस्मा उ ह न ददतुः ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha ha śaunakaṃ ca kāpeyam abhipratāriṇaṃ ca kākṣaseniṃ pariviṣyamāṇau brahmacārī bibhikṣe tasmā u ha na dadatuḥ || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: Once, while Śaunaka Kāpeya and Abhipratārin Kākṣaseni were being served their meal, a student of sacred knowledge begged of them. They gave him nothing.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: A second narrative tests the doctrine: the learned diners are about to be examined on the very vidyā by a beggar at their door.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The Lord sends the student to expose knowledge held without humility.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The refused alms set the stage for the boy's riddle on the Absorber.

verses.devanagari: स होवाच महात्मनश्चतुरो देव एकः कः स जगार भुवनस्य गोपास्तं कापेय नाभिपश्यन्ति मर्त्या अभिप्रतारिन्बहुधा वसन्तं यस्मै वा एतदन्नं तस्मा एतन्न दत्तमिति ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: sa hovāca mahātmanaś caturo deva ekaḥ kaḥ sa jagāra bhuvanasya gopās taṃ kāpeya nābhipaśyanti martyā abhipratārin bahudhā vasantaṃ yasmai vā etad annaṃ tasmā etan na dattam iti || 6 ||
verses.english_translation: He said: 'One god — who is he? — has swallowed four mighty ones, he the guardian of the world. Him, O Kāpeya, mortals do not see, O Abhipratārin, though he dwells manifoldly. Truly, to him to whom this food belongs, it has not been given.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The riddle: the one Absorber (prāṇa/Vāyu) has swallowed the four great gods (fire, sun, moon, waters — or speech, eye, ear, mind). Food withheld from any feeder is withheld from the universal Eater present in all.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The hidden guardian who eats through every mouth is the Lord as universal sustainer; to refuse the beggar is to refuse Him.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The boy names Vāyu-prāṇa, the world-guardian under Viṣṇu, dwelling unseen in all eaters; alms denied to him insult his Master.

verses.devanagari: तदु ह शौनकः कापेयः प्रतिमन्वानः प्रत्येयायात्मा देवानां जनिता प्रजानां हिरण्यदंष्ट्रो बभसोऽनसूरिर्महान्तमस्य महिमानमाहुरनद्यमानो यदनन्नमत्तीति वै वयं ब्रह्मचारिन्नेदमुपास्महे दत्तास्मै भिक्षामिति ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: tad u ha śaunakaḥ kāpeyaḥ pratimanvānaḥ pratyeyāyātmā devānāṃ janitā prajānāṃ hiraṇyadaṃṣṭro babhaso 'nasūrir mahāntam asya mahimānam āhur anadyamāno yad anannam attīti vai vayaṃ brahmacārin nedam upāsmahe dattāsmai bhikṣām iti || 7 ||
verses.english_translation: Reflecting on this, Śaunaka Kāpeya approached him and said: 'He is the self of the gods, the begetter of creatures, the gold-toothed devourer, truly wise. Great, they say, is his majesty, for unconsumed he eats even what is not food. Thus, O student, do we meditate on this.' (Then he said:) 'Give him alms!'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The diner redeems himself by expounding the Absorber's majesty: uneaten eater, self of gods. Recognising the boy's knowledge, he honours it with food — knowledge acknowledged restores dharma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Śaunaka's hymn describes the Lord's universal feeding; right understanding immediately issues in generosity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The 'gold-toothed devourer' is prāṇa empowered by Viṣṇu; the wise diner, seeing this, serves the beggar as the god's representative.

verses.devanagari: तस्म उ ह ददुस्ते वा एते पञ्चान्ये पञ्चान्ये दश सन्तस्तत्कृतं तस्मात्सर्वासु दिक्ष्वन्नमेव दश कृतं सैषा विराडन्नादी तयेदं सर्वं दृष्टं सर्वमस्येदं दृष्टं भवत्यन्नादो भवति य एवं वेद य एवं वेद ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tasma u ha dadus te vā ete pañcānye pañcānye daśa santas tat kṛtaṃ tasmāt sarvāsu dikṣv annam eva daśa kṛtaṃ saiṣā virāḍ annādī tayedaṃ sarvaṃ dṛṣṭaṃ sarvam asyedaṃ dṛṣṭaṃ bhavaty annādo bhavati ya evaṃ veda ya evaṃ veda || 8 ||
verses.english_translation: So they gave him alms. Now these five (wind, fire, sun, moon, waters) and those five (breath, speech, eye, ear, mind), being ten, make the kṛta (the perfect throw). Therefore in all the quarters the ten — food itself — are the kṛta. This is Virāj, the eater of food. By her all this is beheld. He who knows thus beholds all this and becomes an eater of food — yes, he who knows thus.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: The two pentads unite as the perfect ten — the winning throw that gathers all, echoing the dice-image of the opening story. Virāj, the cosmic totality, is the eater; the knower shares her universal vision and sustenance. The repetition closes the Saṃvarga-vidyā.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The tenfold Virāj is the Lord's cosmic form as universal enjoyer; the meditator beholds all things in her and lacks no nourishment.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: The ten powers form the Lord's perfect order; he who knows their unity under Viṣṇu receives abundance and vision.
